water_temp_read.read: Water temperature is in a favorable trout range.

light_pressure_read.read: Light and pressure do not strongly push the outlook either way.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

generated_text.quick_read: Trout fishing on the Chattahoochee River is very good with ideal water temperature and recent stocking supporting good action. Expect trout to favor riffles, runs, seams, and structure using nymphs and attractor patterns.
generated_text.best_window: The morning of day 2 is best due to ideal water temperature and lower wind speeds creating favorable conditions for trout activity and feeding near riffles and runs, optimizing presentation success.
generated_text.current_conditions: Water temperature is 57°F with flow at 1510 cfs, gage height 2.93 ft, and turbidity 21 NTU.
generated_text.weather_setup: Sky cover is around 22% with stable pressure at 30.00 inHg and neutral pressure trend. Winds average 4.4 mph from the southwest with a moderate chance of light rain at 0.02 inches expected.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will hold in comfortable feeding lanes close to cover, favoring riffles, runs, seams, and structure. Use clean drifts with smaller natural nymphs and stocked-trout attractor flies like egg patterns, squirmy worms, and duracells. Balanced nymph approaches with midges and soft hackles are recommended, avoiding overly aggressive presentations.
